coredata.dc:description: Developing novel and efficient multimodal nanomaterial-based anticancer agents to meet complex clinical demands is an urgent challenge. Herein, novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped Cu2S@ZIF-8 yolk-shell Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) was firstly constructed by a simple and mild wet-chemical method at room temperature. The MOFs possessed exiting photothermal performance and catalytic activities, which were further enhanced greatly upon near infrared light irradiation or in tumor microenvironment (TME) due to their unique composition and structure. The possible mechanism was discussed. The introduction of GQDs played a key role because it could effectively absorb NIR light and convert partial Cu2S nanodots to CuS nanodots, thus further increase NIR light absorption and generate cascaded electron transport effects, which will enhanced the photothermal performance and catalytic activities of yolk-shell MOFs. After small molecules drugs doxorubicin (DOX) and folic acid (FA) were loaded and conjugated, the as-prepared MOFs could be expected as efficient nanoplatform for cervical cancer synergistic therapy. These prominent intelligent laser and tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive features of yolk-shell nanocomposite provide an innovative paradigm to explore nanoplatform for tumor therapy.

head.ce:title: Near infrared light and tumor microenvironment multi-sensitive “Yolk-Shell MOF Nanoreactor” for synergistic treatment of cervical cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and efficient yolk-shell MOFs with unique structure and properties was prepared, for first time, which could be used as nanoreactors for synergistic treatment of cervical cancer. Upon the NIR irradiation, there is a magic conversion of the structure, composition and property in MOFs, which could greatly enhance its photothermal performance and catalytic activities, and cascaded electron transport and electric field enhancement effects were proposed to this behavior.

Developing novel and efficient multimodal nanomaterial-based anticancer agents to meet complex clinical demands is an urgent challenge. Herein, novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped Cu2S@ZIF-8 yolk-shell Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) was firstly constructed by a simple and mild wet-chemical method at room temperature. The MOFs possessed exiting photothermal performance and catalytic activities, which were further enhanced greatly upon near infrared light irradiation or in tumor microenvironment (TME) due to their unique composition and structure. The possible mechanism was discussed. The introduction of GQDs played a key role because it could effectively absorb NIR light and convert partial Cu2S nanodots to CuS nanodots, thus further increase NIR light absorption and generate cascaded electron transport effects, which will enhanced the photothermal performance and catalytic activities of yolk-shell MOFs. After small molecules drugs doxorubicin (DOX) and folic acid (FA) were loaded and conjugated, the as-prepared MOFs could be expected as efficient nanoplatform for cervical cancer synergistic therapy. These prominent intelligent laser and tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive features of yolk-shell nanocomposite provide an innovative paradigm to explore nanoplatform for tumor therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors declare that the main data supporting the findings and conclusions of this study are available within the article. Extra data are available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, regarded as a primary threat to human health and critical barrier to improve life expectancy worldwide, has been commonly bothering people for many years [1,2]. Although various traditional cancer therapy modalities including surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy are available, there is still facing insurmountable challenges such as unfavorable tumor metastasis, severe side effects, thelackofselectivity and drug resistance[1,2]. Therefore,novel therapeutic strategiesarestillneededto achieve effective and safety cancer therapy. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, nanomaterial-based photothermal therapy (PTT) and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) have gained tremendous attention due to their many advantages including high specificity, minimal invasiveness, and precise spatial and temporal selectivity over conventional cancer therapies [3–14]. In detail, PTT is an emerging method, which could produce hyperthermia at tumor site under laser irradiation utilizing typical nanomaterial or nanocomposite with outstanding photothermal conversion performance as photothermal agents. PTT is used to induce apoptosis and/or necrosis of tumors and achieve ideal curative effect. The photothermal conversion efficiency of these nanomaterials or nanocomposites determined the final curative effect. Thus, the fabrication of these nanomaterials with high photothermal performance is crucial [3–8]. Similarly, there is another novel noninvasive and targeted cancer treatment, CDT, which kill precisely cancer cells by excessive highly toxic hydroxyl radical (·OH) from metal-related to Fenton catalytic reaction or Fenton-like catalytic reaction in TME, simultaneously without damage on normal tissue, for the reason of Fenton catalytic or Fenton-like catalytic reaction is substantially suppressed in a normal microenvironment [9–14]. The catalytic activity of nanomaterial in CDT is essential for the final outcomes. Single-functional nanomaterials employed in PTT or CDT alone always cannot achieve the desired anti-tumor effect due to the inherent limitations of tumor including generation of heat shock protein against hyperthermia, low catalytic activity suffering from insufficient H2O2 and H+ in TME. And, emerging studies have proved the synergistic antitumor effect of PTT combined with CDT [15–19]. This combination therapy not only could synchronously kill cancer cells by hyperthermia of PTT and reactive oxygen species (ROS) of CDT to enhance the efficiency of cancer therapy, but also enhancing the production of ROS by PTT during the period of therapy. The increased generation of ROS further promoted apoptosis and necrosis of cancer cells, thus enhanced the efficiency of cancer therapy. Therefore, combination therapy was proposed [15–21]. Typically, all-in-one nanomedicine strategies are proposed for improving anticancer outcome [15–24]. It is important to emphasize that the design and construction of multifunctional nanocomposite played a decisive role in the process of efficient treatment in the all-in-one nanomedicine strategies, which could effectively combine different components with manifold activity into one platform. Various nanocomposites were prepared and applied for highly effective treatment of diverse cancer [15–24]. Among these nanocomposites, yolk-shell nanostructures have been the focus of attention due to their advantages of controllable structure and typical confinement effects, which have been used as nonreactors for various catalytic reactions and cancer therapy [25–31]. However, most of yolk-shell nanoparticles in early reports are single-core structure and the core is small and simple, which led to low catalytic performance due to insufficient confinement effects utilization and lack of synergistic effect among different components. How to construct multifunctional yolk-shell nanocomposite with several cores to achieve synergy remains a challenge, as it requires reasonable choice and precise regulation of each component with different inherit features.

Copper-based nanomaterials could act as Fenton catalytic reagents for CDT and photothermal conversion agents for PTT [32–37]. For instance, copper sulfide (CuS) nanomaterials possessed high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) in the NIR-II region, which could realize deeply penetrating mild PTT for deep solid tumor; Cu2+ can also play a role in reducing GSH and produce Cu+, and the generated Cu+ could catalyzed Fenton-like reaction to produce lots of harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the weakly acidic a maximum reaction rate 1 × 104 M−1 s−1, which is ∼ 160-fold faster than that of Fe2+ [38–40]. However, Cu+ is toxic to living cells due to the induced ROS, it is not suitable to be directly used as nanotheranostics for cancer treatment. In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were also regarded as ideal candidate for effective cancer therapy [41–49]. GQDs also were employed to PTT and CDT for cancer due to due to their peroxidase (POD)-like activity and high photothermal conversion efficiency [41–49]. GQDs have photoluminescence (PL) with high quantum yield, excellent solubility and environment-friendly preparation, which make them as ideal imaging agents for cancer diagnosis [41,42,46,48,49]. However, the short retention time in vivo due to their ultrafine size limited its further application. In view of this, it might be a good idea to integrate them with the other biocompatible nanomaterial. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), particularly zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), have been investigated as one of functional units of nanotheranostics due to their high biocompatibility, large surface area, porosity, controllable chemical functionality and TME-sensitive characteristics [50–59]. Chemotherapy drugs are easily loaded on and sustained release from numerous pores in ZIF-8, because ZIF-8 is stable at pH 7.4 and decomposes in TME. Based on these advantages, ZIF-8 is expected as an ideal shell for the construction of the nanocomposite for cancer treatment. Various ZIF-8 based nanocomposites were synthesized and reported for PTT/CDT or chemotherapy by loading DOX because doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) is used widely in chemotherapy for cancer [50–59]. When DOX is loaded into ZIF-8, it can be sustainable released into cancer cells, thus decreasing side effect of chemotherapy. However, yolk-shell nanocomposite combined with ZIF-8, graphene quantum dots, DOX and copper sulfide (Cu2S or CuS) haven't been discussed and reported. Synthesizing and constructing such yolk-shell nanocomposite remains a huge challenge.

Based on the above discussion, herein, we fabricated firstly a GQDs doped Cu2S@ZIF-8 yolk-shell nanocomposite for synergistic therapy of cervical cancer. As shown in Scheme 1 , a simple and mild one-pot method was used to construct GQDs and Cu2+ doped ZIF-8 (GCZ). Then the as-prepared GQDs doped Cu2S@ZIF-8 yolk-shell nanocomposite were obtained by introduction of NaHS into the suspensions of GQDs and Cu2+ doped ZIF-8 (GCSZ). It was found that NIR laser irradiation could enhance greatly the photothermal and catalytic performance of GCSZ, which can be attributed to the introduction of GQDs in MOFs. When NIR light was employed, GQDs can absorb NIR light and promote the formation of GQDs@Cu2S@CuS by partially oxide of Cu2S nanodots in GCSZ (the partially oxide of GCSZ is denoted as POGCSZ), thus generate cascaded electron transport effects among GQDs, Cu2S, CuS, which will greatly suppress the rate of recombination of e-/h+ pairs, ultimately enhancing the catalytic performance. And the emerging CuS nanodots also further enhanced the photothermal of MOFs owing to the inherit photothermal conversion feature of CuS. Then, folic acid (FA) was conjugated into and the DOX as the model drug for chemotherapy was loaded on the ZIF-8 shell to enhance the efficiency of therapy and active-targeting effect (DFPOGCSZ) because the FA receptors are overexpressed on the surface of the cervical cancer cells. Folic acid (FA) molecules could thus be used to target these cells. In vitro or in vivo, DOX (chemotherapy drugs), GQDs, Cu2S, CuS (nano-agents of PPT and CDT) were sustained released, following by destruction of ZIF-8 shells in the mild acidic TME. Firstly, cascaded Fenton catalytic reaction and persistent CDT were triggered by these CDT nano-agents (GQDs@Cu2S@CuS) in TME. Subsequently, PTT was also achieved employing the heat generated by the GQDs, Cu2S and CuS after irradiated by 808 nm or 1064 nm laser irradiation (NIR I or NIR II), which could further kill tumor cells and enhance CDT and chemotherapy. As a result, the as-prepared yolk-shell MOFs could efficiently inhibit the growth of tumors both in vitro and in vivo through synergistic therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material and methods
Materials and apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: All the inorganic salts and surfactant used in the construction of the as-prepared MOFs were all analytically pure. And all reagents in vitro and in vivo experiments were used as received without further purification. The all were listed in S1 in the Supplementary Materials. Various electron microscopic and spectral apparatuses were employed to characterize the as-prepared MOFs, which were listed in S2 in the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of yolk-shell GQDs doped Cu2S@ZIF-8 (GCSZ) and partial oxidation of GCSZ (POGCSZ)
Synthesis of GQDs and Cu2+ co-doped ZIF-8 (GCZ)
ce:section.ce:para: 99 mg (Zn(NO3)·6H2O, 20 mg Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 2 mL GQDs (1 mg/mL) dispersions were successively mixed into 20 mL methanol with magnetic stirring for 20 min, which was defined as solution A. 0.21 g 2-methylimidazolium and 0.37 g PVP dissolve in 10 mL of methanol, which was defined as solution B. Then, solution B was added into solution A with magnetic stirring for 2 h. The final product was washed by centrifugation several times and dissolved in 4 mL methanol, which was GQDs and Cu2+ co-doped ZIF-8, abbreviating as GCZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of yolk-shell GQDs and Cu2S co-doped ZIF-8 (GCSZ)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.4 mL (10 mg/mL) of NaHS·xH2O and 0.1 m l methanol was successively added into 0.5 mL GCZ (8 mg/mL) methanol solution and reacted for 30 min. The as-prepared samples were washed by centrifugation several times and stored for use

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs@Cu2S@CuS@ZIF-8 by partial oxidation of GCSZ (denoted as POGCSZ) and fabrication of DFPOGCSZ
ce:section.ce:para: The stored GCSZ dispersions (5 mg/mL, 4 ml) was partially oxidized when it was irradiation under 808 nm laser with 1.5 W/cm2 power for 30 min, which was partial oxidation of yolk-shell MOFs, denoting as POGCSZ. After that, 12 mg POGCSZ was added the as-prepared 10 mL FA-DMSO solution (containing 12 mg FA, 12.6 mg EDC and 9.4 mg NHS) and reacted for 12 h. Then, it was collected through centrifugation and washed with water, which was folic acid modifying POGCSZ, denoting as FPOGCSZ. For DOX loading, 6 mg FPOGCSZ was ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL of DOX tris-buffer (1.2 mg/mL, pH=8.5), and the mixture was continuously stirred for overnight reaction in dark. Finally, the DOX-loaded FPOGCSZ were collected by centrifugation at 1,000 rpm for 10 min, which was denoted as DFPOGCSZ. Almost all added DOX were loaded into the as-prepared POGCSZ. Its loading efficiency was also calculated approximately 98.3 % in weight defined by the maximum absorption measurements of DOX on 480 nm (The detailed method of calculation in supplementary materials, S3). The pH-triggered and laser triggered release behaviors was also investigated at different pH (5.0 and 7.4) and various time intervals by UV–vis absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effect of the as-prepared MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: NIR laser (808 nm and 1064 nm, Beijing Laserwave OptoElectronics Technology Co., Ltd) and IR camera (Fotric 225) were employed to monitor the photothermal performance of the as-prepared MOFs. The dispersions of different samples (1.0 mL, deionized water; GQDs doped ZIF-8 (GZ); GQDs and Cu2+ co-doped ZIF-8 (GCZ); yolk-shell GQDs@Cu2S co-doped ZIF-8 (GCSZ)) or 1.0 mL GCSZ with different concentrations were irradiated by NIR laser and the temperatures were recorded on IR camera. Subsequently, five “laser on–off” cycles experiments on were also carried out to investigate the photothermal stability of GCSZ. Next, the photothermal behavior of POGCSZ was investigated using the same procedures. Finally, the photothermal stability and conversion efficiency (η) of the POGCSZ was also investigated and calculated by a reported method in S4 in supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of the as-prepared nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the enhanced catalytic activity of the prepared POGCSZ, 3,3′,5,5 −tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was adopted as probe to testify its catalytic activity. Typically, various nanocomposites were added into in 3 mL PBS buffer solution (pH=5.0, containing 30 μL 0.1 M TMB and 30 μL H2O2 (w.t. 30 %)). The final concentration of these nanocomposites was 16 μg/mL. Afterward, the solution was incubated in the dark for 5 min at room temperature for further photographing and UV–vis testing. And the catalytic activity was also investigated by varying pH value of POGCSZ dispersion solution. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was also used to evaluate the ·OH of the as-prepared nanocomposites. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was added into 1 mL as-prepared nanocomposites PBS solution (100 μg/mL, pH 5.0, H2O2:10 mM). Finally, the ESR signals were collected with an ESR instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-cancer experiments of as-prepared nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake features and fluorescence imaging of the DFPOGCSZ were confirmed by TEM and confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). For TEM imaging, Hela cells were seeded into cell culture dish. After incubation with NPs for 6 h, the cells were harvested and sliced into 70 nm ultrathin sections, the samples were imaged using a TEM instrument. For fluorescence imaging of the DFPOGCSZ in cells, Hela cells were seeded into confocal dishes, and were captured by CLSM after treated with DFPOGCSZ for 6 h.

The cytotoxic experiments were investigated through a traditional Cell Counting Kit (CCK8) method. Firstly, normal cell line (mouse normal neuroglial cell line BV2) was adopted to prove the biocompatibility and biosafety of the as-prepared MOFs. These normal cells were incubated with POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ under various concentrations for 24 h in 96-well plates, then Cell Counting Kit was added and continued to incubate for another 4 h and recorded on a microplate Reader at 450 nm. Then, the cytotoxic experiments of cancer cells were also performed to prove the anti-cancer performance of the as-prepared MOFs using the same method, excepting that the Hela cells was used instead of normal cells. Next, Calcein AM (green fluorescence)/propidium iodide (PI, red fluorescence) live/dead stain experiments were carried out to the therapeutic properties of POGCSZ in vitro. After incubated with POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ for 24 h, Hela cells were incubated with Calcein AM/PI for 3 h, which was observed by CLSM. Finally, the intracellular ROS experiments to prove the CDT effect of the as-prepared nanocomposites using DCFH-DA probe, with observations also made under CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-Cancer mechanism of as-prepared MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-cancer mechanism of MOFs was comprehensively explored through flow cytometry assay, and western blot experiment. For flow cytometry, Hela cells were seeded into 6-well plate overnight for cell attachment (5 × 105 cells/well). After treated with DFPOGCSZ (100 μg/mL) and irradiated by 1064 nm laser for 12 h, Annexin V-FITC/PI or Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) assay kit was added, then the apoptosis cells, dead cells, and related ROS expression of Hela cells were recorded by flow cytometry (Becton Dickinson FACSCanto II).

For western blot, Hela cells were seeded into cell culture dish. After treated with modified and drug-loaded LDFPOGCSZ (100 μg/mL), Hela cells were harvested and lysed in whole cell lysates (KeyGEN), the western blot technique was utilized to analyze the protein expression (autophagy-related protein LC3, and β-actin) (see S5 in supplementary materials).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-cancer performance of as-prepared nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: BALB/c mice (female, 18–20 g, 6–8 weeks old, Jicui Yaokang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, Nanjing, China)) were adopted and injected with Hela cells to establish animal tumor models in experiments. All procedures agree with the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care and Treatment Committee of Xuzhou Medical University (202209S016). Mice were randomly divided into 6 groups: Ctrl group (injected with saline only), L1064 group (injected with saline and treated with 1064 nm laser), POGCSZ group (injected with POGCSZ only), DFPOGCSZ (injected with DFPOGCSZ only), LPOGCSZ group (injected with POGCSZ and irradiated by 1064 nm laser), LDFPOGCSZ group (injected with DFPOGCSZ and irradiated by 1064 nm laser). All groups were injected through the tail vein at a dose of 5 mg/kg (including saline considered as the control) and observed 30 days and sacrificed to harvest tumors at the 30 days. Weight of mice and tumor parameters (volume and weight) were observed and recorded every 5 days. After 30 days, all mice were euthanized. The main tissues were harvested for hematoxylin, eosin (H&E), Ki67 staining and the blood were collected for blood biochemistry analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of the as-prepared yolk-shell GCSZ
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the GQDs doped (GZ) and GQDs, Cu2+ co-doped ZIF-8 (GCZ) nanopolyhedrons with the size of ～80 nm and 90 nm were synthesized using the classical co-precipitation method, respectively (Fig. 1 a,b). Subsequently, HS- etched part of the ZIF-8 in GCZ nanopolyhedrons when NaHS was added, resulting in the formation of GQDs@Cu2S co-doped ZIF-8 (GCSZ) yolk-shell structure by the inward collapse of the structure of ZIF-8 and formation of cuprous sulfide by reduction of copper ions Fig. 1c. Similar variations on morphology was also found in the SEM image (Fig. 1d–f), where the solid polyhedral structures gradually were transformed into the yolk-shell structures. Further, the composition details were also investigated by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) characterization techniques. The EDS and EDS line profile spectra in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 showed that the yolk-shell MOFs were composed of C, N, O, Zn, Cu and S elements. Meanwhile, the elemental mapping clearly revealed that the yolk was composed of C, N, O, Zn, Cu and S, while the shell was composed of C, N, O, Zn in Fig. 1g. Apparently, the yolk was formed due to the inward collapse of ZIF-8 caused by the formation of cuprous sulfide. The HRTEM imaging demonstrated that the yolk is an aggregation of GQDs nanodots (～1-2 nm), Cu2S nanodots (～2-3 nm) and ZIF-8 nanoparticles in Fig. 1h. No obvious lattice was found in ZIF-8. However, the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm were observed in Fig. 1h-1j, which are attributable to the (002) surface of the GQDs and Cu2S crystal in early reports, respectively [60,61]. Thus, uniform yolk-shell MOF (GCSZ) nanocomposite with GQDs and Cu2S as cores were fabricated.

The details of the construction of yolk-shell GCSZ were also investigated by XRD, UV–vis, Zeta and XPS in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, all diffraction peaks of GCZ are in line with the simulated ZIF-8, which indicates that incorporation of low content of Cu2+ and GQDs do not affect the crystalline structure of ZIF-8 [62,63]. However, typical peaks of Cu2S (PDF No:33–0490) in Fig. 2a were observed in GCSZ [64,65]. The UV–vis and zeta potential in Fig. 2b, c also proved the transformation from GCZ to GCSZ due to the presence of NaHS. In Fig. 2b, the NIR absorption from 800-1100 nm of GCSZ is obvious greater than that of GCZ, indicating vulcanization of Cu2+, which could enhance the NIR absorption of the MOFs [66]. Furthermore, a peak at 676 nm were observed in GCSZ, which may due to the particularly yolk-shell structure of GCSZ because the peak disappeared when the yolk-shell structure was destroyed in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4 when the reaction time was prolonged. Similarly, zeta potential in Fig. 2c was observed from 8.2 mV of GCZ to −9.7 mV of GCSZ further confirmed the vulcanization of Cu2+ when NaHS was added. XPS analysis was consequently carried out to investigate the detail of the chemical composition of GCSZ. The XPS survey spectrum of GCZ presented in Fig. 2d clearly shows the C, N, Zn, O, Cu elements were included. In contrast, besides these elements, a new S element was found after the introduction of NaHS, which accounts for the transformation from copper ion of Cu2S. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of the as-prepared yolk-shell nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 2e. Three devolved peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV and 287.9 eV were found, which is attributed to C=C, C-C/C–OH and C=O groups, respectively. The results further proved the existence of GQDs in GCSZ [67]. The binding energies at 932.26 eV and 952.19 eV in Fig. 2f are corresponded to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 of Cu+ respectively. The results are consistent with the previous reports, where the copper in sulfide minerals is usually in the form of mono-valence [64,68]. No peaks of shake-up satellites of Cu2+ is present, indicating the formation of Cu2S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the as-prepared MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the after mentioned discussion, as-prepared of MOFs are expected as photothermal agents in cancer therapy due to the presence of GQDs and Cu2S. We investigated the photothermal generation efficiency of the as-prepared GCSZ. The photothermal NIR images of various nanocomposites irradiated with 808 nm NIR laser (H2O, GZ, GCZ and GCSZ) were recorded by IR camera. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, negligible temperature changes of water upon 808 nm laser irradiation were seen. It was clear that the photothermal effect of GCSZ was superior to that of GCZ and GZ. Quantificationally, the temperature change of 16.4℃ (GCSZ), 5.3℃ (GCZ) and, 2.7℃ (H2O) and 2.1℃ (GZ) was observed in Fig. 3b. Though GCSZ demonstrated the best photothermal performance, the performance wasn’t perfect when concentration of GCSZ is low and laser power is low in Fig. 3c and Fig. S5. Thrillingly, we found that NIR laser irradiation could greatly increase their photothermal conversion efficiency of GCSZ. As shown in Fig. 3d, the photothermal conversion efficiency of GCSZ is gradually increased in the initial 5 cycles of laser irradiation, which might be due to the formation of GQDs@Cu2S@CuS and partial oxidation of Cu2S (partial oxidation of GCSZ is denoted as POGCSZ). The photothermal conversion became relative stable after continuous irradiation for 30 min in Fig. 3e. Even at a relatively low concentration (200 μg/mL), there was still 18.6 ℃ increased, which show excellent photothermal performance in Fig. 3f. Furthermore, in addition to 808 nm photothermal response, the as-prepared MOFs (POGCSZ) also showed the fascinating photothermal performance under 1064 nm irradiation in Fig. 3g, h and Fig. S6, which could achieve deeper penetration depth and better therapeutic effect for solid tumor in vivo than that of 808 nm. And, the ascending temperature profile (T) of POGCSZ (1.6 mg/mL) in the presence of 1064 nm laser over 35 min, including heating and the natural cooling is illustrated in Fig. 3i to demonstrate its excellent photothermal performance. The η values of the as-prepared POGCSZ were calculated to be 44.1 % according to the equation in early reports and the results in Fig. 3i, Fig. S7 and Fig. S8 [69,70], which is superior to 35.5 % under 808 nm irradiation in Fig. S9. In addition to outstanding photothermal performance, amazing peroxidase-like catalytic activity was also observed in POGCSZ. As shown in Fig. 3j, the intensity of characteristic peak the oxidized TMB in POGCSZ is obvious higher of that in GCSZ, GCZ and GZ, which indicated that NIR laser irradiation could enhance the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of MOFs. In addition, only acidic conditions could activate the catalytic activity of MOFs as compared to almost no catalytic activity in neutral conditions in Fig. 3k, which will reduce the side effects caused by the produced ROS and enhance the targeting for cancer due to the acidic microenvironment features of tumors. The highest intensity of electron paramagnetic resonance signals in Fig. 3l also proved more ·OH was trapped by TMPO when the POGCSZ as catalyst, confirming the enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity of POGCSZ.

For the sake of further discuss the intrinsic mechanism of NIR laser irradiation responsive process of MOFs, a series of experiments on including XRD, UV–vis, TEM, SEM, EDS-mapping and XPS analysis also was performed after laser irradiation. After laser irradiation, the yellow GCSZ became black POGCSZ in Fig. S10. And the UV–vis absorption in NIR region became strong as shown in Fig. S7, which may be due to oxide of Cu2S nanodots. XRD result further showed the existence of Cu2(I)S and Cu(II)S in black POGCSZ in Fig. S11. TEM and SEM images in Fig. 4 a, b indicated that yolk-shell structures were converted into hollow structures scattering with many nanodots. EDS-mapping in Fig. S12 proved that the cores (GQDs@Cu2S@CuS) diffused outward and evenly distributed in the shell. In XPS spectra, there is no change in the number of elements of GCSZ after laser irradiation in Fig. S13. However, in Cu 2p high resolution spectrum, six Cu 2p peaks including Cu 2p3/2 at 932.3 eV, 934.3 eV with the satellite peak at 940.6 eV and the Cu 2p1/2 at 951.8 eV, 954.6 eV with the satellite peak at 956.6 eV, 963.1 eV were found (Fig. 4c), which clearly stated the coexistence of Cu+ and Cu2+. Compared to only Cu+ in POGCSZ before NIR irradiation, the ratio of Cu+ and Cu2+ was 1.89 in POGCSZ according to on relative atomic mass in XPS analysis software. We also found it was difficult to oxidation of Cu+ to Cu2+ even after a long irradiation, which may be due to the protection of shell. In addition, the signals of O1s became more evident and the peak profile of S2p broadens in Fig. 4d, and e, illustrating that the chemical states of sulfur become manifold after the as-prepared GCSZ was irradiated by laser. These results were in agreement with the previous reports of Cu2(I)S and Cu(II)S [71]. Obviously, laser irradiation drove the cores to spread out to shell exposing to more oxygen, which could surmount inherent defects of insufficient photothermal conversion efficiency of traditional yolk-shell structures due to outer shell hiding the heat transfer. And the CuS nanodots were formed by the oxide of Cu2S nanodots, which would greatly improve that photothermal performance due to its outstanding NIR light absorption capacity. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4f and g, it was emphasized that GQDs played a key role in the enhancement of catalytic and photothermal performance of the as-prepared MOFs. On one hand, in presence of GQDs, there was electrons transfer between GQDs and Cu2S in the yolk-shell MOFs due to the reported difference of CB between GQDs (−0.5 to −1.0 eV, vs NHE) and Cu2S (−0.45 eV, vs NHE) [72–74]. Furthermore, GQDs possessed excellent abilities to absorb NIR light, which would promote electrons transfer between GQDs and Cu2S when NIR light was introduction, results in efficient charge separation and greatly suppresses the rate of recombination of e-/h+ pairs, thus enhancing photothermal performance and the catalytic performance [75]. On the other hand, the absorbed NIR light by GQDs could further trigger the oxide of Cu2S and the formation of CuS, which was an outstanding photothermal conversion agent. Furthermore, a new electrons transfer interface was formed between Cu2S and CuS owing to their reported different CB (Cu2S: −0.45 eV and CuS 0.59 eV, Fig. 4g), which would further increase the photothermal performance and catalytic activity of the as-prepared MOF by cascaded mode [76]. Furthermore, the spatial electric-field distribution was theoretically modeled by the finite-element method (FEM) in Fig. 4g. It is can be seen that the enhanced spatial electric-field distribution in hollow MOF sphere (right) than that of solid MOF sphere (left) also resulted in increased heat generation when the laser irradiation was introduced [76,77]. Obviously, the excellent photothermal performance and catalytic activity of POGCSZ were expected as efficient therapeutics for enhanced PTT and CDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and release properties
ce:section.ce:para: MOFs are often used for drug delivery and drug release due to their porous properties and large specific surface. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured to analyze the textural properties of the POGCSZ, which demonstrated a type I isotherm and large specific BET surface area (758.979 m3/g) due to the presence of microspores accessible to the N2 molecules in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S14. Evidently, POGCSZ offers much more drug loading capacity, it’s up to the large cavity. A cytotoxic chemotherapy drug, DOX, was loaded on POGCSZ after FA modification to realized chemotherapy and increase targeting function. Comparing to the POGCSZ, a new typical peak ranging centered at 480 nm and obvious change on Zeta potential was observed in Fig. 5b and c, indicating the successful FA modification and DOX loading. The pH and laser sensitive release behaviors of DOX were examined in PBS at pH 7.4 and 5.0 with or without the introduction of 1064 nm laser irradiation. In Fig. 5d，handful of DOX (12.6 % in 24 h and 19.3 % in 72 h) were released at physiological condition (pH=7.4). However, when the pH dropped to 5.0, the cumulative release reached dramatically to 29.1 % in 24 h and 51.1 % in 72 h, respectively. When pH dropped to 5.0, POGCSZ will be destructed due to the features of MOF, this led to release more DOX from the hollow cavity. It was worth noting that DOX was released to 31.2 % in 24 h and 55.7 % in 72 h on the condition of 1064 nm laser irradiation at pH 5.0. The main reason of DOX released acceleration is that the heat produced by 1064 nm laser irradiation, leading to dissociate GQDs@Cu2S@CuS aggregates and the Zn-O coordination in the ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic properties of POGCSZ in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the amazing photothermal and catalytic features of the as-prepared MOFs, it was anticipated to be as a novel efficient nanoplatform for cancer therapy. Prior to cancer therapy, biocompatibility of nanomaterial is a prerequisite. The biocompatibility of POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ was examined by the BV2 cells by CCK8 assay, respectively. It was found that the survival rate of BV2 was still more than 90 % even at a relatively high concentration (400 μg/mL) in Fig. 5e after the cells were co-incubated with different concentrations of POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ for 24 h, respectively. Obviously, POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ had excellent biocompatibility, which was suitable for biomedical utilizations. Then, the therapeutic effect of the as-prepared MOFs in vitro was investigated by the co-incubation Hela cells with POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ under different conditions, respectively. It was easily found from Fig. 5f that the group of the cells cultivated with POGCSZ alone or DFPOGCSZ showed a limited cytotoxic effect in 24 h (87.0 % cell survived with POGCSZ and 69.7 % cell survived with DFPOGCSZ with 400 μg/mL concentration). Cytotoxicity for Hela cells in DFPOGCSZ was higher than that of POGCSZ due to the chemotherapy effect of DOX. In contrast, POGCSZ exhibited significant cytotoxicity under 1064 nm laser irradiation (LPOGCSZ group), the cell viability decreased to 9.7 % at a high concentration (400 μg/mL), which could be ascribed to the high efficiency of PTT of the MOFs. It was worth noting that Hela cells in DFPOGCSZ with 1064 nm laser irradiation groups (LDFPOGCSZ group), the cell viability was nearly 6.3 % at the highest concentration of DFPOGCSZ (400 μg/mL), Obviously, the combination of chemotherapy, CDT and PTT was achieved. To visualize the anticancer efficacy of the as-prepared MOFs in vitro, we also confirmed DFPOGCSZ efficient uptake in the cytoplasm by Hela cells in Fig. 5g and Fig. S15. Calcein-AM/PI double staining kit was adopted and used to visualize live/dead cell in Fig. 5h. It could be found that the control group (Ctrl) and 1064 nm laser irradiation alone (L1064) group caused negligible cell death. The red signal was increased slightly in POGCSZ group due to a small amount of ·OH from CDT. The red signals (dead cells) were increased significantly in DFPOGCSZ group due to the introduction of chemotherapy effect from the released DOX in acidic tumor microenvironment (TME) of cancer cells. Number of dead cells (red signals) in POGCSZ with 1064 nm laser irradiation group (LPOGCSZ group) was augmented greatly, indicating exciting photothermal anti-cancer efficiency of the as-prepared MOFs. The most remarkable thing was that almost all cells in DFPOGCSZ with 1064 nm laser irradiation groups (LDFPOGCSZ group) were killed, which was consistent with the results of the corresponding CCK8 assay. These findings further proved the outstanding photothermal conversion performance of the as-prepared MOFs and provided a plausible method to cancer therapy. In addition, experiments of generation of intracellular ·OH in Fig. 5i and Fig. S16 were also validated the catalytic activity of the as-prepared MOFs. Comparing to the groups without nanocomposite (Ctrl group and L1064 group), obvious green signals or strong fluorescence signals were observed in all groups with MOFs. Furthermore, laser irradiation could enhance the intensity of green fluorescence of intracellular ·OH, which also manifested the existence of synergistic effect between the photothermal and catalytic performance in the as-prepared MOFs. When 1064 nm laser was instead with 808 nm laser, the same trends were also obtained in Figs. S17–S19.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of anti-tumor of the as-prepared MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: Although many nanocomposites have been used as nanoplatform for anti-cancer, their mechanisms of action are still unclear. Understanding the mechanisms underlying the anti-tumor property of nanocomposite is crucial for their further application. In this context, various experiments were conducted in Fig. 6 . Apoptosis and necroptosis are the main modes of cell death. We firstly used flow cytometry assay to detect the cell death modality of Hela mediated by DFPOGCSZ with 1064 nm laser irradiation. It was shown that the dead Hela cells increased in LDFPOGCSZ group, but barely changed of the apoptosis cells in Fig. 6a and b. The results indicated that the MOFs did not cause cell death by apoptosis. To further study the death of Hela cells caused by MOFs and laser irradiation, the Hela cell ultrastructural were observed by use of transmission electron microscope (TEM). As a results, we found much more autophagosomes in LDFPOGCSZ group than the other groups, which was shown in Fig. 6c and d. Additionally, another important indicator of autophagy are acidic vesicular organelles (AVOs) in cells. Thus, we also stain the LDFPOGCSZ-treated Hela cells with acridine orange (AO) to certified whether autophagy induced by DFPOGCSZ and 1064 nm laser irradiation. Consistent with the results of TEM observation, cytoplasmic AVO formation in LDFPOGCSZ group cells was quickly observed compared with control group, which was shown in Fig. 6e and f. This finding further revealed that DFPOGCSZ and 1064 nm laser irradiation increased cell autophagy in Hela cells. At last, in protein level, autophagy-related protein LC3 is originally characterized as an autophagy-associated protein, and the conversion of LC3-I to LC3-II is required for autophagosome formation, that indicates the initiation of autophagy. Thus, LC3-II conversion was detected by immunoblotting assay to confirm the causal relationship between autophagy and LDFPOGCSZ treated Hela cells Fig. 6g. The treatment with DFPOGCSZ and 1064 nm laser irradiation induced much more accumulation of LC3-II in Hela cells, and the results were coincident with the TEM observation and AO staining. In summary, DFPOGCSZ and 1064 nm laser irradiation induced Hela cell death via autophagy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic properties of the as-prepared nanocomposite in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Given the high efficiency of PTT in vitro, the photothermal performance in vivo was also evaluated in Fig. 7 . Temperature change of the tumor region was recorded by a thermal camera, and the results were found in Fig. 7a and b. No distinct temperature of tumor was observed in Ctrl, POGCSZ and DFPOGCSZ groups because these groups were either no nanocomposites or no laser irradiation. Several degrees of temperature increase were observed in L1064 group due to the generation of a small amount of heat from laser irradiation. In sharp contrast, the rapid temperature increased in LPOGCSZ and LDFPOGCSZ group was found, which could effectively kill tumor cells. Obviously, the as-prepared MOFs showed satisfactory photothermal performance in vivo, which could be used as photothermal nano-agents for cancer therapy. Subsequently, the anti-cancer effects of the MOFs in vivo were further discussed in Fig. 7c–f. Mice were randomly divided into 6 groups (Ctrl group, L1064 group, POGCSZ group, DFPOGCSZ group, LPOGCSZ group, LDFPOGCSZ group) as depicted in Materials and methods section. The final images of tumor-bearing mice after treated in different groups and the corresponding tumor images were shown in Fig. 7c and d. It was clear that the tumors were becoming smaller and even disappearing after treated with the MOFs under different conditions. Typically, in LDFPOGCSZ group, the mice were injected with POGCSZ and irradiated by 1064 nm laser), tumors in four mice vanished after 30 days treatment. The statistical results of the weight and volume (V/V0) changes of tumors in various groups were also discussed in Fig. 7e and f. A trend of LDFPOGCSZ group < LPOGCSZ group < DFPOGCSZ group < POGCSZ group < Ctrl group, L1064 group could be found, indicating excellent synergistic therapeutic effect of the MOFs. Ki67, as a marker protein, is widely used in assessing the proliferative fraction in tumors. Then, we stained the tumors with Ki67 and H&E staining to clarify the anti-tumor effects of the MOFs. It was shown less Ki67 staining and more damaged cells in LDFPOGCSZ group mice tumors than those of the control group mice in Fig. 7g, h and Fig. S20. Finally, the biosafety of the as-prepared MOFs was investigated by H&E staining, blood routine indexes and blood biochemistry parameters in Fig. 8 and Fig. S21. Negligible damage or inflammatory lesion on major organ (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were found in Fig. 8a, proving the as-prepared MOFs had good biocompatibility. Comparing to control group, the blood routine indexes and blood biochemistry parameters in LDFPOGCSZ group in Fig. 8b–g and Fig. S21 showed no obvious deleterious effects in blood biochemical indexes (WBC: white blood cells; RBC: red blood cells; AST: aspartate aminotransferase ALB: albumin; ALT: alanine aminotransferase; CREA: creatinine; TP: total protein UREA: urea), that further confirmed the biosafety of the as-prepared MOFs. Taken together, it was concluded that all experiments in vivo further proved the as-prepared yolk-shell MOFs were potential ideal candidate for application in cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped Cu2S@ZIF-8 yolk-shell Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) was successfully fabricated by a simple wet-chemical method, for the first time. Outstanding photothermal conversion ability and catalytic activities were achieved upon NIR laser and under acidic condition due to its unique composition and structure. The introduction of GQDs and the relevant NIR activating multi-components electron transport interface of GQDs@Cu2S@CuS resulted in the cascaded electron transport and increase on NIR absorption, which respond for the amazing photothermal performance and catalytic activities of yolk-shell MOFs. Experiments in vitro and in vivo were preformed employing Hela cells and the corresponding tumor bearing mice as treating models. The results showed that more than 90 % cervical cancer cells were killed in vitro and the tumor almost disappeared when the as-prepared MOFs as therapeutic agent combining with CDT, PTT and chemotherapy. This study could be used in drug delivery or other possible applications, simultaneously offer a feasible method for construction of novel MOF-based yolk-shell nanocarriers. Furthermore, the yolk-shell MOFs also offers more insights into synergistic treatment of solid tumors, with excellent promise.
